message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "Competitive landscape and market analysis for AI-powered recruitment platforms targeting solo/independent recruiters and small recruiting agencies (teams of 5-10), with a freemium model (free individual tier + paid team tier).

Research these dimensions specifically:
1. Existing recruitment platforms (ATS, recruiting CRM, applicant tracking) that target solo recruiters and small agencies — e.g. Recruit CRM, Loxo, Crelate, Manatal, JobAdder, Zoho Recruit, Bullhorn, RecruiterFlow, Vincere, iSmartRecruit, Ceipal, Tracker, Hireflow, hiring tools like Workable, Ashby, Greenhouse (note which are NOT for small/solo). For each relevant one: target customer, pricing/tiers, free tier availability, AI features, key strengths/weaknesses.
2. Which of these use a freemium or free-tier model (Zoho Recruit free plan, etc.) and how they monetize. How "easy to start" / self-serve onboarding is positioned.
3. Where AI is being applied in recruiting today (resume parsing, candidate matching/ranking, sourcing, outreach email generation, interview scheduling, candidate Q&A, job description writing, chrome extensions for sourcing from LinkedIn). Which features are table-stakes vs differentiators in 2025-2026.
4. The pain points of small/independent recruiters: data scattered across WhatsApp/Excel/Google Drive, time spent on admin, lack of context on past placements, no revenue visibility. What tools claim to solve these and gaps that remain.
5. Pricing benchmarks: typical per-seat/month pricing for recruiting CRM/ATS at the small-agency level, and what free tiers include.
6. Market size and trends for recruitment software / recruiting CRM, especially the SMB / independent recruiter segment."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://recruiterflow.com/blog/ai-screening-tools/
**Title:** Top AI Screening Tools Shaping Hiring Practices in 2026
**Found via:** AI features table-stakes vs differentiator search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the page content first.

input.prompt: Extract key facts relevant to: AI-powered recruitment platforms for solo/small recruiting agencies, AI features in recruiting (resume parsing, candidate matching, screening, sourcing, outreach), which features are table-stakes vs differentiators, pricing/tiers, free tiers, target customers, and market trends. List specific named tools, their pricing, AI features, target customers. Also note the publish date of the article.

content.content:
# AI Recruitment Platforms: Key Facts from Recruiterflow Blog

**Publication Date:** May 30, 2026

## Market Trends

- By 2026, 70% of businesses will use AI for hiring; 82% rely on AI for resume screening
- AI reduces cost-per-hire by 30% and time-to-fill from 44 to 11 days
- Screening costs drop by up to 75% with AI implementation

## Table-Stakes Features (Expected Baseline)

- Resume parsing and automated data analysis
- Bias reduction mechanisms
- Customizable filtering by skills/experience
- Reporting and analytics dashboards
- Integration with existing ATS/CRM systems

## Differentiator Features

- Video interview analysis (HireVue)
- Neuroscience-based assessments (Pymetrics)
- Predictive performance scoring (X0PA AI, Harver)
- AI agents for autonomous outreach and scheduling
- Skill-based matching beyond keyword matching
- Customizable interview question generation

## Named Tools & Pricing

| Tool | Pricing | Target Size | Key AI Feature |
|------|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| Recruiterflow | $119/user/month | All sizes | Resume ranking, interview Q generation |
| HireVue | Custom | Enterprise | AI video interview analysis |
| Harver | Custom | High-volume | Predictive attrition assessments |
| X0PA AI | Custom | Medium-large | Predictive scoring, retention analytics |
| Eightfold.ai | Custom | Enterprise | Skill-based matching, internal mobility |
| HireEZ | Custom | Scale hiring | 750M+ profile sourcing, Agent Mode |
| HireBeat | $169/month | SMB | Resume filtering, async video |
| Workable | $299/month | SMB | Semantic resume scanning |
| CodeSignal | Custom | Tech hiring | AI-assisted coding assessments |
| Sova Assessment | Custom | Mid-large | Custom assessments, video scoring |
| Textio | $6,257/6 months | Enterprise | Job description optimization |

**No free tiers identified in article.**

## Solo/Small Agency Considerations

Recruiterflow and HireBeat highlighted as accessible to smaller operations; most enterprise tools require custom pricing, suggesting higher barriers for solopreneurs.

claims.claim: By 2026, AI-driven resume parsing/screening is positioned as a table-stakes baseline feature in recruiting platforms, with 82% of businesses relying on AI for resume screening, while differentiators are video interview analysis, predictive performance scoring, and autonomous AI agents for outreach/scheduling.
claims.quote: 82% rely on AI for resume screening ... Table-Stakes Features: Resume parsing and automated data analysis ... Differentiator Features: Video interview analysis (HireVue) ... AI agents for autonomous outreach and scheduling ... Skill-based matching beyond keyword matching

claims.claim: Recruiterflow, an ATS/CRM serving recruiting agencies, is priced at $119 per user per month.
claims.quote: Recruiterflow | $119/user/month | All sizes | Resume ranking, interview Q generation

claims.claim: Workable is priced at approximately $299/month and targets SMBs, offering semantic resume scanning AI.

Most enterprise-grade AI recruiting tools (HireVue, Harver, X0PA AI, Eightfold.ai, HireEZ, Textio) use custom/quote-based pricing rather than self-serve transparent tiers, creating a higher barrier for solo recruiters; only a subset like Recruiterflow and HireBeat ($169/month) are positioned as accessible to smaller operations.
claims.quote: Recruiterflow and HireBeat highlighted as accessible to smaller operations; most enterprise tools require custom pricing, suggesting higher barriers for solopreneurs.

claims.claim: AI adoption in hiring is claimed to reduce cost-per-hire by 30% and cut time-to-fill from 44 days to 11 days, with screening costs dropping up to 75%.
claims.quote: AI reduces cost-per-hire by 30% and time-to-fill from 44 to 11 days ... Screening costs drop by up to 75% with AI implementation

content.text: Extracted claims from the Recruiterflow blog post. This is a vendor blog (Recruiterflow markets its own ATS/CRM), so sourceQuality is "blog" — useful for pricing/feature framing but inherently promotional and self-interested, especially where it positions its own product favorably. Key takeaways for the research question: resume parsing is framed as table-stakes while video analysis, predictive scoring, and autonomous AI agents are differentiators; Recruiterflow lists at $119/user/month and HireBeat at $169/month as the small/SMB-accessible options, while most enterprise tools use opaque custom pricing; no free tiers were identified in this article.
